title: DisjointHardInterventions

tex_statement.html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of nodes of $G$. Then the CDMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting $W_2$ is the same CDMG that arises from first node-splitting $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\spl(W_2)} = \lp G_{\spl(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}. \]
tex_statement.env_title: Disjoint hard interventions and node-splittings commute

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_8 -- "DisjointHardInterventions".
%
% Proof lifted from lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex:505--533 (the
% \Claude{...} block immediately following the claim). Only light edits:
% explicit \refrow{def_3_10} and \refrow{def_3_11} cross-links the first
% time \doit and \spl appear in the body, \medskip\noindent\emph{...}
% section headers mirroring claim_3_4_proof_HardInterventionsCommute.tex,
% a \checkmark at the end of each component-agree paragraph, and a closing
% one-liner. The \Claude{...} wrapper from the LN source is dropped.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_8 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: DisjointHardInterventions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_8}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_8}
%
% Cross-references: see claim_<ref>_statement_<title>.tex in this folder
% for the corresponding statement.

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}[Disjoint hard interventions and node-splittings commute]
   Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of nodes of $G$.
   Then the CDMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting $W_2$ is the same CDMG that arises from first node-splitting $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$.
      \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\spl(W_2)} =  \lp G_{\spl(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}.   \]
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
\medskip\noindent\emph{Preamble.}
Since $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ are disjoint, both operations are
well-defined in either order: on the left, $\spl(W_2)$ acts on $G_{\doit(W_1)}$
whose output set $V \sm W_1$ still contains $W_2$ (using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$);
on the right, $\doit(W_1)$ acts on $G_{\spl(W_2)}$, whose input/output universe
contains every vertex of $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ untouched by the split (again using
$W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$). For $w \in W_2$ we write $w^0$ (incoming copy) and
$w^1$ (outgoing copy) for the two split copies of $w$; for $v \notin W_2$ we
identify $v^0 = v^1 = v$. Each side of the displayed equation is a CDMG presented
as a 4-tuple $(J_\ast, V_\ast, E_\ast, L_\ast)$, and we verify the four components
in turn.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Node sets.}
Left: by \refrow{def_3_10}, $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ has input set $J \cup W_1$ and
output set $V \sm W_1$; then $\spl(W_2)$~\refrow{def_3_11} (with
$W_2 \ins V \sm W_1$, by $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$) gives
\[
  J \cup W_1
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^0 \dcup W_2^1.
\]
Right: $G_{\spl(W_2)}$ has input set $J$ and output set $(V \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^0 \dcup W_2^1$;
then $\doit(W_1)$ (with $W_1 \cap W_2^0 = W_1 \cap W_2^1 = \emptyset$, since
$W_2^0, W_2^1$ are fresh split copies of vertices in $W_2$ and $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$)
gives
\[
  J \cup W_1
  \qquad\text{and}\qquad
  (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^0 \dcup W_2^1.
\]
These agree. \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Directed edges.}
Left: $E_{\doit(W_1)} = E \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$; then $\spl(W_2)$
yields
\[
  \lC v_1^1 \tuh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E,\ v_2 \notin W_1 \rC
  \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \mid w \in W_2 \rC.
\]
Right: $E_{\spl(W_2)} = \lC v_1^1 \tuh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \mid w \in W_2 \rC$;
then $\doit(W_1)$ removes the edges with head in $W_1$. An edge $v_1^1 \tuh v_2^0$
has head $v_2^0 \in W_1$ iff $v_2 \in W_1$ (using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$: if
$v_2 \in W_2$ then $v_2^0$ is a fresh split copy not in $W_1$, and if $v_2 \notin W_2$
then $v_2^0 = v_2$). The split edges $w^0 \tuh w^1$ have head $w^1 \notin W_1$,
since $w \in W_2$ and $W_1, W_2$ are disjoint. After removal we obtain
\[
  \lC v_1^1 \tuh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E,\ v_2 \notin W_1 \rC
  \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \mid w \in W_2 \rC,
\]
which agrees with the left-hand side. \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Bidirected edges.}
Left: $L_{\doit(W_1)} = L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$; then $\spl(W_2)$
yields
\[
  \lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L,\ v_1, v_2 \notin W_1 \rC.
\]
Right: $L_{\spl(W_2)} = \lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$;
then $\doit(W_1)$ removes the bidirected edges touching $W_1$. Using
$v_k^0 \in W_1 \iff v_k \in W_1$ for $k = 1, 2$ (same fresh-copy / identity case
analysis as for the directed edges, again using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$), the
result is
\[
  \lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L,\ v_1, v_2 \notin W_1 \rC,
\]
which agrees with the left-hand side. \checkmark

All four components agree, completing the proof.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Lemma — Disjoint hard interventions and node-splittings commute

Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ two disjoint subsets of nodes of $G$. Then the CDMG obtained from first hard intervening on $W_1$ and then node-splitting $W_2$ is the same CDMG that arises from first node-splitting $W_2$ and then hard intervening on $W_1$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\spl(W_2)} = \lp G_{\spl(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}. \]

Proof.

Preamble. Since $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ and $W_2 \ins V$ are disjoint, both operations are well-defined in either order: on the left, $\spl(W_2)$ acts on $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ whose output set $V \sm W_1$ still contains $W_2$ (using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$); on the right, $\doit(W_1)$ acts on $G_{\spl(W_2)}$, whose input/output universe contains every vertex of $W_1 \ins J \cup V$ untouched by the split (again using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$). For $w \in W_2$ we write $w^0$ (incoming copy) and $w^1$ (outgoing copy) for the two split copies of $w$; for $v \notin W_2$ we identify $v^0 = v^1 = v$. Each side of the displayed equation is a CDMG presented as a 4-tuple $(J_\ast, V_\ast, E_\ast, L_\ast)$, and we verify the four components in turn.

Node sets. Left: by def_3_10, $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ has input set $J \cup W_1$ and output set $V \sm W_1$; then $\spl(W_2)$ def_3_11 (with $W_2 \ins V \sm W_1$, by $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$) gives \[ J \cup W_1 \qquad\text{and}\qquad (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^0 \dcup W_2^1. \] Right: $G_{\spl(W_2)}$ has input set $J$ and output set $(V \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^0 \dcup W_2^1$; then $\doit(W_1)$ (with $W_1 \cap W_2^0 = W_1 \cap W_2^1 = \emptyset$, since $W_2^0, W_2^1$ are fresh split copies of vertices in $W_2$ and $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$) gives \[ J \cup W_1 \qquad\text{and}\qquad (V \sm W_1 \sm W_2) \dcup W_2^0 \dcup W_2^1. \] These agree. \checkmark

Directed edges. Left: $E_{\doit(W_1)} = E \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$; then $\spl(W_2)$ yields \[ \lC v_1^1 \tuh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E,\ v_2 \notin W_1 \rC \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \mid w \in W_2 \rC. \] Right: $E_{\spl(W_2)} = \lC v_1^1 \tuh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \mid w \in W_2 \rC$; then $\doit(W_1)$ removes the edges with head in $W_1$. An edge $v_1^1 \tuh v_2^0$ has head $v_2^0 \in W_1$ iff $v_2 \in W_1$ (using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$: if $v_2 \in W_2$ then $v_2^0$ is a fresh split copy not in $W_1$, and if $v_2 \notin W_2$ then $v_2^0 = v_2$). The split edges $w^0 \tuh w^1$ have head $w^1 \notin W_1$, since $w \in W_2$ and $W_1, W_2$ are disjoint. After removal we obtain \[ \lC v_1^1 \tuh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E,\ v_2 \notin W_1 \rC \cup \lC w^0 \tuh w^1 \mid w \in W_2 \rC, \] which agrees with the left-hand side. \checkmark

Bidirected edges. Left: $L_{\doit(W_1)} = L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC$; then $\spl(W_2)$ yields \[ \lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L,\ v_1, v_2 \notin W_1 \rC. \] Right: $L_{\spl(W_2)} = \lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$; then $\doit(W_1)$ removes the bidirected edges touching $W_1$. Using $v_k^0 \in W_1 \iff v_k \in W_1$ for $k = 1, 2$ (same fresh-copy / identity case analysis as for the directed edges, again using $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$), the result is \[ \lC v_1^0 \huh v_2^0 \mid v_1 \huh v_2 \in L,\ v_1, v_2 \notin W_1 \rC, \] which agrees with the left-hand side. \checkmark

All four components agree, completing the proof.

lean.name: subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint
lean.code:
-- Helper required to state the row (discharges the LHS inner-NS precondition in the main theorem's signature).
/-- If `W₂ ⊆ G.V` and `W₁` is disjoint from `W₂`, then `W₂` is
contained in the output set of the hard-intervened graph:
`W₂ ⊆ (G.hardInterventionOn W₁).V = G.V \ W₁`. Used to discharge the
precondition of the inner `nodeSplittingOn W₂` call on the LHS of the
commute identity below. -/
theorem subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint
    {G : CDMG α} {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hdisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    W₂ ⊆ (G.hardInterventionOn W₁).V := by

lean.name: hardInterventionOn_nodeSplittingOn_comm
lean.code:
/-- claim_3_8 (`DisjointHardInterventions`): for a CDMG `G : CDMG α` and
disjoint subsets `W₁ W₂ : Set α` with `W₂ ⊆ G.V`, hard intervention
on `W₁` and node-splitting on `W₂` commute. Mirrors the displayed
equation in the `\Lem` at `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`
line 501. -/
theorem hardInterventionOn_nodeSplittingOn_comm
    {G : CDMG α} {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V) (hdisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₁).nodeSplittingOn W₂
        (subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint hW₂ hdisj)
      = (G.nodeSplittingOn W₂ hW₂).hardInterventionOn (Sum.inl '' W₁) := by

lean_explanation:
The LN states an equality of CDMGs $(G_{\doit(W_1)})_{\spl(W_2)} = (G_{\spl(W_2)})_{\doit(W_1)}$ between two ways of composing hard intervention and node-splitting; the Lean encoding pins this as a literal `Eq` of CDMGs because both sides have the same carrier `α ⊕ ↑W₂` (the carrier of a single node-splitting on $W_2$).

`α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type, matching the LN's silent assumption that $G$ lives over some unspecified vertex set; `G : CDMG α` is $G = (J, V, E, L)$; `W₁ W₂ : Set α` are the two subsets. The hypothesis `hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V` is the LN's $W_2 \subseteq V$; `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is Mathlib's order-theoretic disjointness predicate, which on `Set` unfolds to $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ — the LN's "two disjoint subsets". The LN's third precondition $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ does not appear as a Lean hypothesis; see the design notes.

The bridging helper `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint` packages the small inclusion $W_2 \subseteq V \setminus W_1$ that the LN states parenthetically (`graphs.tex` line 510). It is what discharges the precondition for the inner `nodeSplittingOn W₂` call on the LHS, where the vertex set has already been reduced by the hard intervention to $V \setminus W_1$.

Mathlib idioms worth flagging: `Set.image` (written infix as `''`) lifts a function pointwise to sets, so `Sum.inl '' W₁ : Set (α ⊕ ↑W₂)` is the canonical embedding of $W_1$ into the post-split carrier via the LN's "0-copy" identification of $\alpha$ with $\mathrm{inl}(\alpha)$. `↑W₂` is the coerced subtype `{x // x ∈ W₂}` — Lean's way of carrying the LN's $1$-copies $v^1$ for $v \in W_2$. `Disjoint` is the order-theoretic predicate from Mathlib's lattice library rather than a primitive set predicate, which lets it interoperate uniformly with the rest of the order API.

The theorem takes the implicit data `G W₁ W₂` and the two explicit hypotheses `hW₂` and `hdisj`, and concludes literal `Eq` of two CDMGs over `α ⊕ ↑W₂`. The LHS applies `hardInterventionOn` first (carrier-preserving on `α`) and `nodeSplittingOn` second (extending to `α ⊕ ↑W₂`); the RHS applies them in the opposite order, with the LN's "the same $W_1$" on the outer HI spelled out as the lift `Sum.inl '' W₁` because the outer HI now lives over the post-split carrier.
design_choices:
**Literal `Eq`, not `CDMGEquiv`.** Both sides have carrier `α ⊕ ↑W₂`, definitionally equal across the two compositions: on the LHS `hardInterventionOn W₁` is carrier-preserving (`α → α`) and `nodeSplittingOn W₂` then extends to `α ⊕ ↑W₂`; on the RHS `nodeSplittingOn W₂` extends first to `α ⊕ ↑W₂` and `hardInterventionOn (Sum.inl '' W₁)` then preserves that carrier. No re-labeling bijection is needed, so a literal `Eq` of CDMGs is type-correct. This puts the row in the same regime as claim 3.4 (`HardInterventionsCommute`) and is precisely why claim 3.7 (`TwoDisjointNodeSplittingsCommute`) — which iterates *two* node-splittings and lands on def-distinct carriers — singles out this row as not needing the `CDMGEquiv` machinery. Encoding it as a `CDMGEquiv` regardless was rejected because it would force every call site to compose carrier bijections that are trivially the identity, and would obscure the fact that hard intervention's carrier-preservation absorbs the `Sum.inl`-lift of $W_1$ into the target-set argument of the outer HI on the RHS.

**No `W₁ ⊆ J ∪ V` precondition.** The LN writes $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ as part of its preamble, but `hardInterventionOn` is well-defined for every `W₁ : Set α`, and vertices in $W_1 \setminus (J \cup V)$ are inert: no edge has an endpoint there (by `E_subset` / `L_subset`), so removing them under hard intervention is the identity on all four CDMG data fields. They ride along the LHS = RHS equality without contributing anything, and the commute identity holds for arbitrary `W₁`. The LN's $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ is informal scaffolding to make $\doit(W_1)$ mathematically meaningful in prose — dropping it makes the lemma maximally useful at call sites without weakening it. Carrying the hypothesis anyway was rejected on call-site ergonomic grounds: downstream consumers (notably claim 3.11, the SWIG mirror) often hold only the disjointness fact, and forcing them to also produce $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$ would be busywork.

**`Disjoint W₁ W₂` is load-bearing on both sides, for different reasons.** On the LHS the inner `nodeSplittingOn W₂` operates on `G.hardInterventionOn W₁`, whose vertex set is $G.V \setminus W_1$. Its precondition therefore demands $W_2 \subseteq G.V \setminus W_1$, which is exactly the conclusion of the bridging helper `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint` — consuming `hdisj`. Without disjointness, the bare `hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.V` does not imply this post-HI inclusion and the inner NS call fails to elaborate. On the RHS the lifted target set `Sum.inl '' W₁` lives entirely in the `inl`-half of the post-split carrier, which is semantically correct *only* because $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$: a vertex $w \in W_1 \cap W_2$ would be duplicated by the inner NS into both `Sum.inl w` and `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩`, and `Sum.inl '' W₁` would silently miss the `Sum.inr`-copy. The post-split HI would then strip only one of the two split copies of $w$ — demonstrably different from the LHS, which removes $w$ before NS ever sees it. So `hdisj` is structurally required for typing on one side and for semantics on the other.

**Lift `W₁ ↦ Sum.inl '' W₁` on the RHS's HI argument.** The outer operation on the RHS is hard intervention on the post-split graph, which lives over the carrier `α ⊕ ↑W₂`. Its target set must therefore be a `Set (α ⊕ ↑W₂)`, not a `Set α`. Under the convention `Sum.inl = 0-copy = canonical observation copy` from def 3.11, the LN's "the same $W_1$" *is* the `inl`-image of $W_1$ in the split carrier; the LN identifies $\alpha \cong \mathrm{inl}(\alpha)$ implicitly throughout def 3.11 / def 3.12 ("we make the identification $W = W^0$" at `graphs.tex` line 197), but Lean's stricter type discipline forces us to spell the lift out. We considered narrowing the lift to `Sum.inl '' (W₁ ∩ (G.J ∪ G.V))` to track only the LN-relevant vertices, and rejected it on two counts: by the inertness argument of the previous paragraph, both lifts produce the same RHS CDMG, so the narrower lift gives no provable strengthening; and it would force every call site to thread `· ∩ (G.J ∪ G.V)` through the `W₁` argument just to discharge the intersection. The wider lift `Sum.inl '' W₁` is the cleaner shape on both counts.

**Bridging helper kept public, not `private`.** The helper `subset_hardInterventionOn_V_of_disjoint` could be inlined into the main theorem's signature or marked `private`, but neither is right here. Inlining the inclusion proof in the LHS's `nodeSplittingOn` precondition slot would make the theorem's signature unreadable and force any goal-state perturbation to re-derive it. Keeping it public — rather than `private` to this file — anticipates the SWIG mirror (claim 3.11), which will re-use this helper unchanged: node-splitting and SWIG node-splitting both promote a subset of $V$ away from the output layer in the same way relative to a prior hard intervention on $W_1$.

**Naming `hardInterventionOn_nodeSplittingOn_comm`.** Follows the Mathlib `_comm` convention for commutativity of two operators (`add_comm`, `mul_comm`, `Set.union_comm`), with both operators in the name and ordered left-to-right to match the LHS of the conclusion. Pairing with `hardInterventionOn_comm` (claim 3.4) and `nodeSplittingOn_comm_equiv` (claim 3.7) makes the subsection's three commute lemmas form a uniform `_comm` family. The mirror `nodeSplittingOn_hardInterventionOn_comm` (operators reversed) is technically equivalent but not exposed — consumers can use `.symm` if they want the swap.
